meta.name: Azure Data Fundamentals (DP-900)
meta.description: Foundational Azure data and analytics concepts for the DP-900 certification exam.

cards.definition: Relational data is structured in tables with rows and columns, enforces a schema, and uses SQL. Relationships are modelled with foreign keys. Non-relational (NoSQL) data stores flexible, schema-less data in formats like documents (JSON), key-value pairs, column families, or graphs - suited for high-volume, varied workloads. Azure SQL Database is relational; Azure Cosmos DB is non-relational.
cards.hint: Relational = strict tables and SQL. Non-relational = flexible formats for scale.

cards.definition: A fully managed PaaS relational database built on Microsoft SQL Server. It handles patching, backups, high availability, and scaling automatically. Supports the T-SQL language, and offers serverless and hyperscale tiers for variable or massive workloads. Best for OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) - frequent reads and writes for operational applications.
cards.hint: SQL Server in the cloud, fully managed - you just write queries.

cards.definition: Microsoft's globally distributed, multi-model NoSQL database. Guarantees single-digit millisecond latency and 99.999% availability SLA. Supports multiple APIs: Core (SQL), MongoDB, Cassandra, Gremlin (graph), and Table. Data is partitioned and automatically replicated across Azure regions. Ideal for globally distributed apps needing low latency (e.g., gaming leaderboards, IoT telemetry).
cards.hint: A NoSQL database that spans the globe with near-instant response times.

cards.definition: OLTP (Online Transaction Processing) handles high volumes of short, real-time read/write operations - e.g., inserting an order, updating a balance. Optimised for write performance and normalised schemas. OLAP (Online Analytical Processing) handles complex queries across large historical datasets for business intelligence - e.g., monthly sales by region. Optimised for read performance with denormalised/star schemas.
cards.hint: OLTP = day-to-day operations. OLAP = business analysis and reporting.

cards.definition: An integrated analytics service combining data warehousing (dedicated SQL pools), big data processing (Apache Spark pools), and data exploration into a single workspace. Connects to Azure Data Lake Storage for large-scale storage. Used for enterprise data warehousing and running complex analytical queries across petabytes of data. Formerly called Azure SQL Data Warehouse.
cards.hint: Azure's all-in-one platform for analytics - from warehouse to big data to ML.

cards.definition: A cloud-based ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) and data integration service. It orchestrates and automates the movement and transformation of data from 90+ sources (on-premises, cloud, SaaS) into data stores like Azure Data Lake, Synapse, or Cosmos DB. Pipelines are built visually or via code. Think of it as the data plumbing between your sources and destinations.
cards.hint: The pipeline orchestrator that moves and transforms data between systems.

cards.definition: A massively scalable, hierarchical object storage service built on Azure Blob Storage, optimised for big data analytics. Combines the scalability and cost of object storage with a file system hierarchy for performance. Supports fine-grained access control (RBAC + ACLs) and is the recommended raw data store for Synapse Analytics and Azure Databricks workloads.
cards.hint: A giant, cheap, hierarchical data store purpose-built for analytics workloads.

cards.definition: A real-time analytics service that processes high-throughput streaming data from sources like Azure Event Hubs, IoT Hub, or Blob Storage. Uses a SQL-like query language to filter, aggregate, and join streams in real time. Outputs results to dashboards, databases, or alerts. Used for real-time fraud detection, live dashboards, and IoT monitoring.
cards.hint: SQL queries running live against a non-stop river of incoming data.

cards.definition: A business intelligence and data visualisation platform that connects to hundreds of data sources, transforms raw data, and creates interactive reports and dashboards. Reports can be published to the Power BI Service for sharing and collaboration. Consists of Power BI Desktop (authoring), Power BI Service (cloud publishing), and Power BI Mobile. Part of the Microsoft 365 ecosystem.
cards.hint: Turn spreadsheets and databases into interactive charts your whole organisation can explore.

cards.definition: Batch Processing collects data over a period of time and processes it together in a scheduled job - e.g., end-of-day payroll calculations. High throughput but introduces latency. Stream Processing handles data continuously in real time as it arrives - e.g., credit card fraud detection within milliseconds. Azure services: Azure Data Factory / Synapse (batch), Azure Stream Analytics / Event Hubs (streaming).
cards.hint: Batch = process a pile at scheduled intervals. Stream = process each item the instant it arrives.

cards.definition: Relational databases store data in tables (entities), rows (records), and columns (attributes). Each row represents a single item, and each column defines a data field with a specific data type. This structure enforces consistency and supports SQL queries.
cards.hint: Tables = entities, rows = records, columns = attributes.

cards.definition: A primary key uniquely identifies each row in a table. A foreign key links one table to another, enforcing referential integrity. These relationships form the backbone of relational database design.
cards.hint: Primary = unique ID. Foreign = link between tables.

cards.definition: A database design technique that reduces redundancy and improves data integrity by splitting data into related tables. Common normal forms include 1NF, 2NF, and 3NF. Used primarily in OLTP systems.
cards.hint: Break data into smaller tables to avoid duplication.

cards.definition: The process of combining tables to improve read performance, often used in OLAP and reporting systems. Reduces joins but increases redundancy.
cards.hint: Fewer tables, faster reads - at the cost of duplication.

cards.definition: A common OLAP data model with a central fact table (metrics) connected to multiple dimension tables (descriptive attributes). Optimized for analytical queries and used in data warehouses.
cards.hint: Fact table in the middle, dimensions around it.

cards.definition: A storage format where data is stored column-by-column instead of row-by-row. Greatly improves compression and analytical query performance. Used in Synapse dedicated SQL pools.
cards.hint: Store data by column for fast analytics.

cards.definition: A managed SQL Server instance offering near 100% compatibility with on-prem SQL Server. Supports SQL Agent, cross-database queries, and instance-level features. Ideal for lift-and-shift migrations.
cards.hint: Full SQL Server features without managing the OS.

cards.definition: A fully managed PostgreSQL database service with built-in high availability, backups, and scaling. Supports open-source extensions and is ideal for modern app development.
cards.hint: Managed PostgreSQL with enterprise reliability.

cards.definition: A managed MySQL database service offering automated patching, backups, and scaling. Supports community MySQL features and is commonly used for web applications.
cards.hint: MySQL in Azure, fully managed.

cards.definition: Cosmos DB offers five consistency levels: Strong, Bounded Staleness, Session, Consistent Prefix, and Eventual. They balance latency, availability, and data accuracy. Session is the default.
cards.hint: Five levels - from Strong to Eventual.

cards.definition: Cosmos DB uses logical partitions based on a partition key to distribute data across physical partitions. Good partition key choices ensure even distribution and high throughput.
cards.hint: Partition key = how Cosmos spreads your data.

cards.definition: A fast, collaborative Apache Spark platform optimized for big data and machine learning. Supports notebooks, Delta Lake, and scalable compute clusters.
cards.hint: Spark-based analytics and ML with collaborative notebooks.

cards.definition: An open-source storage layer that brings ACID transactions to data lakes. Supports versioning, schema enforcement, and time travel. Used heavily in Databricks.
cards.hint: ACID transactions on your data lake.

cards.definition: The process of collecting and importing data from various sources into a storage or analytics system. Can be batch or streaming. Tools include Data Factory, Event Hubs, and IoT Hub.
cards.hint: Getting data into your system - batch or real-time.

cards.definition: The process of cleaning, shaping, and enriching data to make it usable for analytics. Includes filtering, joining, aggregating, and standardizing data. Performed in Data Factory, Databricks, or Synapse.
cards.hint: Turning raw data into usable data.

cards.definition: The presentation of data through charts, dashboards, and reports to help users understand insights. Power BI is the primary visualization tool in Azure ecosystems.
cards.hint: Turning data into charts and dashboards.

cards.definition: The set of processes and policies that ensure data quality, security, privacy, and compliance. Includes cataloging, lineage, and access control. Azure Purview (Microsoft Purview) is the governance solution.
cards.hint: Policies and controls that keep data trustworthy and secure.

cards.definition: A unified data governance service that provides data cataloging, lineage tracking, classification, and compliance management across on-premises and cloud data sources.
cards.hint: The data catalog and governance tool for Azure.

cards.term: Data Security: Encryption at Rest and In Transit
cards.definition: Encryption at rest protects stored data using technologies like AES-256. Encryption in transit protects data moving across networks using TLS. Azure services enable both by default.
cards.hint: Data encrypted while stored and while moving.

cards.definition: Policies that determine how long data is stored and when it is moved to cheaper storage tiers. Azure Blob Storage supports Hot, Cool, and Archive tiers for cost optimization.
cards.hint: Keep data as long as needed, then move it to cheaper storage.

cards.term: Structured vs. Semi-Structured vs. Unstructured Data
cards.definition: Structured data fits neatly into tables with defined schemas (SQL databases). Semi-structured data has tags or markers but no fixed schema (JSON, XML). Unstructured data has no predefined format (images, PDFs, audio). Azure services support all three types.
cards.hint: Structured = tables. Semi-structured = JSON. Unstructured = media files.

cards.definition: A modern architecture combining the reliability of data warehouses with the flexibility of data lakes. Supports both structured and unstructured data. Implemented in Azure using Databricks + Delta Lake + Synapse.
cards.hint: Warehouse + data lake in one architecture.

cards.definition: ETL extracts, transforms, then loads data into a warehouse. ELT extracts, loads, then transforms data inside a scalable engine like Synapse or Databricks. ELT is preferred for big data workloads.
cards.hint: ETL = transform before loading. ELT = transform after loading.

cards.definition: A small-footprint SQL engine optimized for IoT and edge devices. Supports streaming, time-series data, and local analytics.
cards.hint: SQL for IoT and edge scenarios.

cards.definition: A managed service for connecting, monitoring, and managing IoT devices. Often used as an ingestion point for streaming analytics pipelines.
cards.hint: The gateway for IoT device data.

cards.definition: A feature that automatically writes Event Hubs streaming data into Azure Data Lake or Blob Storage for batch analytics.
cards.hint: Auto-save streaming data into your data lake.

cards.definition: A technology in Synapse and SQL Server that allows querying external data sources (Data Lake, Blob Storage) using T-SQL without moving the data.
cards.hint: Query external files using SQL.

cards.definition: A pay-per-query SQL engine that queries data directly from Data Lake without provisioning compute. Ideal for exploratory analytics.
cards.hint: Query files in your data lake without a dedicated cluster.

cards.definition: A provisioned, massively parallel processing (MPP) data warehouse for large-scale analytical workloads. Optimized for high-performance SQL queries.
cards.hint: Provisioned data warehouse with MPP power.

cards.definition: A distributed computing architecture where queries are split across multiple compute nodes to process data in parallel. Used in Synapse dedicated SQL pools.
cards.hint: Break big queries into smaller pieces across many nodes.

cards.definition: A managed big data service supporting open-source frameworks like Hadoop, Spark, Hive, and Kafka. Often used for legacy big data workloads.
cards.hint: Managed Hadoop/Spark clusters in Azure.

cards.definition: Azure ML integrates with Synapse, Databricks, and Data Lake to train and deploy machine learning models using enterprise-scale data.
cards.hint: ML models trained directly on your analytics data.

cards.definition: The ability to track the origin, movement, and transformation of data across systems. Microsoft Purview provides automated lineage tracking.
cards.hint: Know where your data came from and how it changed.

cards.definition: The process of organizing and indexing data assets so users can easily discover and understand them. Purview provides a searchable data catalog.
cards.hint: A searchable inventory of all your data assets.

cards.definition: Labeling data based on sensitivity (e.g., public, confidential, highly confidential). Helps enforce security and compliance policies.
cards.hint: Tag data based on how sensitive it is.

cards.definition: A set of properties ensuring reliable database transactions: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability. Critical for OLTP systems.
cards.hint: The reliability rules for database transactions.

cards.definition: A model used in NoSQL systems: Basically Available, Soft-state, Eventually consistent. Prioritizes availability and scalability over strict consistency.
cards.hint: The NoSQL alternative to ACID.

cards.definition: Data indexed by time, often used in IoT, telemetry, and monitoring scenarios. Azure services like Cosmos DB and SQL Edge support time-series workloads.
cards.hint: Data points tracked over time - like sensor readings.

cards.definition: A horizontal partitioning technique that splits large datasets across multiple databases or nodes to improve scalability and performance.
cards.hint: Split big datasets into smaller pieces across servers.

cards.definition: The process of copying data across multiple locations to improve availability and performance. Cosmos DB automatically replicates data globally.
cards.hint: Keep multiple copies of data for reliability.
